plants: Annual Herb
Lives fast and dies with the frost, pouring everything into a single season of growth and seed.
Arborescent Grass
Grass grown to the height of a tree, its blades broad as swords and its stalks thick as wrists.
Bamboo
Hollow-stemmed and fast as rumor, a grove can appear where bare ground stood a season ago.
Biennial Herb
Patient enough to spend its first year just growing, saving all its color and seed for the second.
Cane
Tall stiff stalks standing in dense ranks, jointed like fingers and rattling when the wind picks up.
Forb
A soft broad-leaved plant that fills the spaces between grasses, often the first to show color in a meadow.
Grass
Thin blades rising in countless numbers, bending together under wind like a single living surface.
Herb
Soft-stemmed and temporary, dying back after it flowers but often leaving a scent that lingers.
Perennial Herb
Returns year after year from the same roots, a little larger each time, as if remembering where it left off.
Rush
Round hollow stems standing stiff in shallow water, marking where the wet ground begins.
Sedge
Looks like grass until you feel the three-sided stem. It grows where the soil stays damp and rich.
